confirmed.title: risk_overrides can silently override the UI-saved sector/position caps at decide-time, and there is NO frontend UI to view or clear an active override
confirmed.summary: decide_trades reads the caps via risk_overrides.get_effective('paper_max_per_sector', settings.paper_max_per_sector) (304) and risk_overrides.get_effective('paper_max_positions', settings.paper_max_positions) (337). An active override in handoff/risk_overrides_audit.jsonl is returned in preference to the .env/UI value. A backend router exists to set/clear (paper_trading.py:579/597/618), but frontend/src/lib/api.ts has NO getRiskLimits/setRiskLimit/clearRiskLimit function and no Manage-tab UI surfaces or clears it — so an operator cannot discover or clear a shadowing override from the app.
confirmed.failure_scenario: At some point an override is written — via a one-off script, an away-ops POST to /api/paper-trading/risk-limits, or a prior test — e.g. set_override('paper_max_per_sector', 2). It persists across restarts (replayed from the JSONL audit on RiskOverrideState init, risk_overrides.py:92-118). The operator later raises 'Max positions per sector' to 5 in the UI; PUT succeeds and .env shows 5, but decide_trades still evaluates max_per_sector=2 because get_effective returns the override first (portfolio_manager.py:303-305). The UI shows 5, the engine enforces 2, and there is no in-app way to see the divergence or clear it. Task notes the audit file is currently EMPTY (latent, not the active cause today), but it is a live footgun that reproduces S1 the moment any override lands.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add getRiskLimits/setRiskLimit/clearRiskLimit to api.ts and surface effective-vs-configured values (GET /api/paper-trading/risk-limits already returns both) in the Manage tab with a 'Clear override' button; render a visible warning banner when any override is active. Consider having the Settings PUT for paper_max_per_sector/paper_max_positions auto-clear the matching risk_override (or at least warn) so the two control surfaces cannot silently diverge.

verdict.reasoning:
All code citations are accurate and verified. (1) portfolio_manager.py:303-305 and 336-338 do read the sector/position caps via risk_overrides.get_effective(key, settings.X); get_effective (risk_overrides.py:134-141) returns an active override in preference to the settings default, and overrides replay from the JSONL on init (risk_overrides.py:92-118) so they survive restart. (2) grep across frontend/src confirms there is NO risk-limits UI and no getRiskLimits/setRiskLimit/clearRiskLimit in api.ts — zero references to /api/paper-trading/risk-limits. (3) The Manage tab saves paper_max_positions/paper_max_per_sector via updateSettings -> PUT /api/settings/ (manage/page.tsx:95,237-238; api.ts:251-252), a DIFFERENT write path than the override JSONL, and settings_api.py PUT never clears the matching override — so a silent divergence is structurally possible.

However, the harmful failure scenario is NOT reachable through the live app today: the audit file handoff/risk_overrides_audit.jsonl does not even exist, so get_effective always returns the settings default and the UI-saved cap IS enforced. There is no in-app path to create an override (the frontend never calls the PUT /risk-limits route); an override can only land via an out-of-band curl/away-ops POST, a one-off script, or a test. The precedence (override wins) is the deliberate phase-49.1 design, not a bug, and the backend GET /risk-limits already exposes effective_value per key (paper_trading.py:590) — only the frontend wiring is missing. This is therefore a genuine but latent observability/robustness gap, not the cause of the reported S1 ('can't change max positions per sector') symptom, which the finding itself concedes. Verdict PLAUSIBLE because the code facts are confirmed but the end-to-end divergence path requires an override precondition that is neither present nor creatable via the app. Severity corrected P2 -> P3: latent, out-of-band-only trigger, by-design precedence, backend observability already exists, and it explains none of S1/S2/S3.

confirmed.title: APScheduler cron hour is captured once at startup; changing paper_trading_hour needs a restart (position caps are NOT affected, ruling the scheduler out as the S1 cause)
confirmed.summary: _add_scheduler_job reads settings.paper_trading_hour to build the cron trigger (1305) and is only invoked at lifespan startup (init_scheduler via main.py:268) and inside the /start endpoint (91). PUT /api/settings/ never re-adds the job, so a changed paper_trading_hour does not reschedule without a restart. Verified that this staleness does NOT extend to the position/sector caps: the job function _scheduled_run (1327) and run_now (1029) both call get_settings() fresh and thread it into decide_trades, so paper_max_per_sector / paper_max_positions ARE picked up per cycle on the clean path.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Operator changes the daily-run hour in settings and expects the next cron to fire at the new time; APScheduler keeps the trigger built from the startup value until the backend is restarted (or /start is hit while no job exists). This is a genuine stale-capture but is scoped to scheduling time only — it does not explain the reported inability to change the sector/position caps, which are re-read fresh each cycle. Included to close the 'does APScheduler hold a stale settings object?' question: yes for the cron HOUR, no for the trading-logic caps.
confirmed.fix_sketch: On PUT /api/settings/ when paper_trading_hour changes (and paper_trading_enabled), call _add_scheduler_job(get_settings()) again (replace_existing=True already handles idempotency) so the cron reschedules without a restart; or reschedule the existing job's trigger in place.

verdict.reasoning: The APScheduler stale-capture mechanism is fully verified in code. _add_scheduler_job (paper_trading.py:1299-1322) builds the cron trigger from settings.paper_trading_hour at line 1305 at add-job time; the trigger then recomputes next_run from that captured hour and never re-reads settings. It is invoked from only two sites: init_scheduler (1295) at lifespan startup (main.py:268), and the /start endpoint (91) — and /start only calls it when no job exists (guard at line 90: `not _scheduler.get_job(...)`), so it is a no-op once the startup job is present. PUT /api/settings/ (settings_api.py:445-450) does get_settings.cache_clear() + reload but has ZERO scheduler references (grep empty), so it never reschedules; a changed hour does not take effect until restart (or /stop then /start). The counter-claim is also correct: _scheduled_run (1327) and run_now (1029) both call get_settings() fresh, and decide_trades reads paper_max_per_sector (portfolio_manager.py:304) and paper_max_positions (:337) off the passed-in fresh settings via risk_overrides.get_effective — so the caps ARE re-read per cycle and the scheduler is correctly ruled out as the S1 cause. One narrowing caveat: paper_trading_hour exists in settings.py:368 but is NOT in the writable SettingsUpdate model (settings_api.py:156-170 omits it), so the literal 'change the hour in the settings form' path is not reachable via the UI/PUT; the hour changes only via a direct .env edit + cache-clear or restart. The stale-capture is nonetheless real for any non-restart change of the hour. The finding is honestly self-scoped as P3 and explicitly not the reported symptom's cause; P3 (polish, no money-path/diversification impact) is correct.

confirmed.title: Momentum-only candidate funnel truncated to top-5 analyzed: no BUY candidates from non-momentum-leading sectors are ever generated
confirmed.summary: rank_candidates ranks the whole universe by a pure-momentum composite and returns only top_n=10 (screener.py:299-303, :483-484); the loop then analyzes only the top paper_analyze_top_n=5 NEW names (autonomous_loop.py:837-838), and the lite trader's BUY gate is ALSO momentum (autonomous_loop.py:2199). There is no per-sector quota, round-robin, or minimum-spread anywhere in candidate selection, so in a single-sector momentum regime every analyzed candidate is one sector and no cross-sector BUY candidate is ever produced.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Semiconductors are the momentum leaders (as now). The top 10 ranked candidates are all semis; only the top 5 new semis get analyzed; each leans BUY only if momentum_20d>3 AND momentum_60d>5 (autonomous_loop.py:2199), further biasing to the hot sector. The sector count cap (paper_max_per_sector=2) then blocks all but 2, and because no other-sector name was ever analyzed, the portfolio can only ever hold ~2 Technology names. BQ confirms: 31 of 31 BUYs in history are Tech/semiconductors (AMD, MU, SNDK, DELL, STX, INTC, TER, COHR, WDC, KEYS, plus KR Hynix/Samsung); zero non-Tech names ever entered the analyzed set or the book. All sector logic in the system is restrictive (caps); none is promotional, so diversification can only 'emerge' from candidate diversity that the momentum ranker structurally cannot provide.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add sector-diversity enforcement to the analyze slice: e.g., cap candidates-per-sector when building analyze_tickers (round-robin across the top-K sectors), and/or decouple paper_analyze_top_n from paper_screen_top_n so more sectors reach the analyzer, and/or reserve some analyze slots for the best candidate in under-represented sectors. Optionally expose a sector-diversity knob. Requires the post-rank sector enrichment (autonomous_loop.py:744-761) to run BEFORE the analyze slice so the slice can be sector-aware.

verdict.reasoning: Attempted to refute; the code fully supports the mechanism. VERIFIED IN CODE: (1) rank_candidates (backend/tools/screener.py:297-303, sort+truncate at :483-484) ranks the universe by a momentum composite (mom_1m*0.40+mom_3m*0.35+mom_6m*0.25, RSI/vol penalties) and returns top_n=paper_screen_top_n (default 10). Overlays are multiplicative adjustments on a momentum base; the genuinely diversifying sector_neutral re-score (:450-474) is default-OFF and sector_momentum_ranks (:354-356) BOOSTS momentum-leading sectors (worsens concentration). The only non-momentum entry point is the news_only injection (:415-428), fixed at composite 5.5, easily out-ranked in a hot-sector regime. (2) autonomous_loop.py:837-838 slices analyze_tickers = new_candidates[:paper_analyze_top_n] (default 5). (3) portfolio_manager.py:174-184 builds buy_candidates EXCLUSIVELY from candidate_analyses, i.e. analyses of exactly those top-5 momentum names (autonomous_loop.py:966-970) — a new BUY can only originate from the top-5 momentum-ranked new tickers. (4) All sector logic is restrictive: the per-sector cap (portfolio_manager.py:303-369) only blocks and queues overflow to a SAME-SECTOR swap path (:460-481); there is no round-robin/quota/minimum-spread anywhere in candidate selection. LIVE-PATH: this is the scheduled daily cycle (operator: 60 trades / 2 positions). BQ grounding on financial_reports.paper_trades: 18 of 20 distinct historical BUY tickers are semiconductor/tech (DELL, MU, SNDK, SK-Hynix, AMD, STX, WDC, INTC, TER, COHR, ON, Samsung, LITE, CIEN, GLW, KEYS, HPE, LG), confirming the single-sector momentum regime premise. DISCREPANCIES (do not refute): line 2199 is a soft LLM 'lean BUY' instruction, not a deterministic gate, and runs only on the lite/fallback path (lite_mode OFF today); and two non-tech BUYs (GEV, FIX, 1 each) exist historically, so the finding's absolute 'never/zero' phrasing is slightly overstated. But the substantive claim — the momentum funnel plus only-restrictive sector logic structurally produces a near-monoculture and cannot generate cross-sector BUY candidates in a single-sector regime — is exactly what the code does and directly explains S2. Severity P1 is correct per the rubric (materially suppresses diversification); it is a genuine design-gap root cause of the reported diversification symptom, not P0 (it does not silently drop all trades or nullify a setting) and clearly above P2/P3.

confirmed.title: Lite analyzer silently forces HOLD on any rail/parse failure; parse-fail default score=5 evades the score-leg of the degraded guard
confirmed.summary: In _run_claude_analysis, a claude-code rail failure sets text="" (:2234) and any missing/non-matching JSON falls to `analysis = {"action":"HOLD","confidence":0,"score":5,...}` (:2250). The Gemini mirror does the same (:2476/:2478). A HOLD never trades, so every degraded analysis silently suppresses a possible BUY, and the score=5 default dodges the score==0 leg of _degraded_scoring_check.
confirmed.failure_scenario: The Max/Claude-Code rail flakes (memory: 'direct-API credits degraded'). claude_code_invoke raises ClaudeCodeError -> text="" -> re.search finds no JSON -> forced HOLD score 5, confidence 0. Only a per-ticker WARN is logged. Result: the ticker can never BUY that cycle. Because score is 5 (not 0), the degraded-scoring guard's score leg misses it (only the confidence==0+UPPER leg can catch it), so cycles of flaky-rail HOLDs can pass under-alarmed. This is the mechanism behind the all-semiconductor HOLD wall in BQ.
confirmed.fix_sketch: On rail/parse failure, do NOT synthesize a tradeable-shaped HOLD with a benign score; return a degraded marker (like phase-61.2 _degraded) that _run_and_persist_one drops, or set score/confidence to a sentinel that unambiguously trips the degraded-scoring guard. Never let an empty-text response become a confident HOLD.

verdict.reasoning:
The defect is real and verified in code. In `_run_claude_analysis` a claude-code rail failure sets `text=""` (autonomous_loop.py:2234); the JSON regex then misses and control falls to the synthesized `{"action":"HOLD","confidence":0,"score":5,...}` at :2250 (Gemini mirror identical at :2476/:2478). This path IS reachable in the live loop even though lite_mode is OFF: it is the full-orchestrator fallback at :1727 (`_select_lite_analyzer(settings.gemini_model)`), entered on the :1714 except whenever `run_full_analysis` raises/returns empty. Default `gemini_model="claude-sonnet-4-6"` (settings.py:30) routes to the Claude lite analyzer, and project memory (direct-API credits degraded; phase-60.1 "9 days at 100% fallback"; phase-66.1 "~162 doomed calls/cycle for 3 weeks") shows the fallback is heavily exercised. When the rail is down, the full path and the lite fallback fail on the SAME cc_rail, so the compound failure is a single correlated root cause, not two independent low-probability events.

The forced HOLD then (a) suppresses a possible BUY — it carries no `_degraded` marker (that dict only exists when `paper_synthesis_integrity_enabled` is ON, which is default OFF/DARK), so `_run_and_persist_one:963` folds it into `candidate_analyses`, and `decide_trades` at portfolio_manager.py:183 does `if rec not in _BUY_RECS: continue`, dropping the ticker; and (b) evades the degraded-scoring guard. In `_degraded_scoring_check` (1959-1986) `score_zero` is `float(final_score)==0`, and final_score=5 so it is False. The finding says only the confidence==0+UPPER leg could catch it, but that leg cannot fire either: the returned top-level dict (2344-2367) has NO `confidence` key (confidence=0 is nested inside full_report.analysis), so `a.get("confidence")` is None and `conf_zero_upper` is False. Neither leg fires — the guard is fully blind to it, worse than the finding claims. BQ grounding (financial_reports.analysis_results, last 21 days) shows the predicted artifacts exist: 4 rows recommendation='HOLD' final_score=5.0 plus 1 'Hold'/5.0, versus 82 HOLD/0.0 rows (a separate path the score-leg DOES catch) and only 2 BUYs total — consistent with the low-throughput symptom.

Severity: I downgrade P1 -> P2. The mechanism is confirmed but its live blast radius is small (~5 score=5 rows vs 82 score-0 HOLDs from a different path), the trade direction is fail-safe (a HOLD never mis-executes), and it is a secondary contributor to S3/S2 rather than the dominant cause. Its real harm is observability/masking — a rail-failure HOLD published as a confident score-5 neutral that dodges the degraded guard and trusted by digest readers. That is a genuine robustness/observability defect worth fixing (the fix_sketch of returning a `_degraded` marker or a sentinel score is correct), but it does not rise to P1. The all-semiconductor HOLD wall is only partly attributable here; most of it is the score-0 path, which the guard already catches.

confirmed.title: Only 5 new candidates deep-analyzed per cycle, drawn from an all-Technology momentum cluster
confirmed.summary: analyze_tickers = new_candidates[:settings.paper_analyze_top_n] with paper_analyze_top_n=5 (settings.py:367) and paper_screen_top_n=10 (settings.py:366). This hard-caps how many fresh BUY signals can be generated per cycle; combined with the ~98% HOLD yield it makes 0-buy cycles the norm. The candidate set is also sector-homogeneous, so the few analyzed rarely span new sectors.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Each cycle analyzes at most 5 new tickers plus reevals. BQ shows the analyzed universe is a repeating semiconductor/tech cluster (MU, AMD, DDOG, SNDK, DELL, INTC, MRVL, STX, 000660.KS, 009150.KS). If the momentum screener's top-5 are all extended tech names that analyze to HOLD, the cycle produces 0 buys and 0 diversification. Raising analyze_top_n is the single biggest lever for 'as many trades as possible', but only helps once the all-HOLD output (findings 1-2) is fixed.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Raise paper_analyze_top_n (e.g., 10-15) and paper_screen_top_n, and add sector diversification to the screener/ranker so the analyzed set is not a single-sector cluster (directly attacks S2 as well). Verify cost headroom against the caps (see session-budget finding) before raising.

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly in code. autonomous_loop.py:838 is `analyze_tickers = [c["ticker"] for c in new_candidates[:settings.paper_analyze_top_n]]`, and settings.py:367 sets paper_analyze_top_n default=5 (paper_screen_top_n=10 at :366). This sits inside run_daily_cycle (line 124), the live APScheduler daily cycle. The full path is confirmed: universe -> screen_universe -> rank_candidates(top_n=paper_screen_top_n=10, strategy="momentum" per screener.py:252) -> sector enrichment -> meta-score -> filter held tickers (new_candidates, line 837) -> slice to top-5 (line 838) -> deep-analyze only those 5. So at most 5 fresh names get a deep analysis (and a chance to emit a BUY) per cycle. The screener is momentum-based (screener.py:194-197), so in a semis/tech-led regime the top-momentum names cluster in Technology, making the 5-name analyzed set sector-homogeneous -- consistent with the cited BQ cluster (MU/AMD/DDOG/SNDK/DELL/INTC/MRVL/STX/000660.KS/009150.KS). The failure scenario is real: if the 5 momentum-extended tech names all resolve to HOLD, the cycle produces 0 buys and 0 new-sector exposure. Could not read backend/.env to confirm the effective value (permission-blocked) but there is no evidence of an override and the default 5 stands. Severity held at P1, not P0: this is a functioning, operator-adjustable design knob rather than a bug -- it does not break the money path or silently drop trades. It is honestly a SECONDARY lever (the finding itself concedes raising it only helps once the ~98% all-HOLD yield is fixed), but it still materially caps per-cycle fresh-signal throughput (S3) and, via momentum-only ranking, sector spread (S2), which is exactly the P1 definition. Not downgraded to P2 because it directly and materially bounds the two stated operator symptoms rather than being a pure correctness/robustness nit.

confirmed.title: Hidden per-cycle session budget ($1.00) is HALF the operator-visible daily cost cap ($2.00), and breaches are swallowed silently
confirmed.summary: _SESSION_BUDGET_USD defaults to 1.0 (:90, env PYFINAGENT_SESSION_BUDGET_USD) while paper_max_daily_cost_usd is 2.0 (settings.py:371). _check_session_budget raises BudgetBreachError with NO log (:99-105); it is raised inside _run_and_persist_one (:925) which runs under asyncio.gather(return_exceptions=True) (:966-970), so the exception is filtered out as a non-dict result. Once cumulative cost hits $1.00, all later-analyzed tickers are silently dropped with no candidate and no log.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Operator raises paper_max_daily_cost_usd (the visible knob) to get more analyses per cycle; the hidden $1.00 session budget still caps cumulative LLM spend at half that, silently truncating the analysis fan-out -> fewer candidates -> fewer buys, with zero explanation in logs or summary. Not currently binding (analysis_cost_today ~$0.23-0.70/cycle) but becomes the active ceiling the moment full-pipeline costs rise or analyze_top_n is raised.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Make the session budget derive from / not be lower than paper_max_daily_cost_usd, or expose it as a real setting. Log a WARNING when _check_session_budget trips (it currently raises silently), and surface budget_truncated on the cycle summary so truncated cycles are auditable.

verdict.reasoning:
All six mechanical claims are verified in source. _SESSION_BUDGET_USD defaults to 1.0 (autonomous_loop.py:90, env PYFINAGENT_SESSION_BUDGET_USD); paper_max_daily_cost_usd defaults to 2.0 (settings.py:371). _check_session_budget (:95-105) raises BudgetBreachError with zero logging. It is called at :925 inside _run_and_persist_one; the local try/except at :926-928 re-raises with no log. That coroutine is dispatched via asyncio.gather(return_exceptions=True) at :966-969 and :973-976, so the raised exception is captured as a result element, and the filter at :970 / :977 (isinstance(r, dict)) silently discards it. Nothing logs the drop and no budget_truncated flag is set on the summary.

Both accumulators reset per cycle (total_analysis_cost=0.0 at :192, _session_cost=0.0 at :208), so within one cycle the $1.00 session budget is genuinely the tighter, quieter ceiling that trips before the $2.00 'daily' cap. The asymmetry is real: the visible daily-cost path (:929-934) logs a WARNING and returns None gracefully, while the session-budget path raises silently and is swallowed. There is also a design-intent divergence: the comment at :927 and the cycle-level handler at :1436-1448 expect BudgetBreachError to propagate and halt the cycle with status='budget_breach', but return_exceptions=True defeats that, so the intended clean halt degrades into unlogged per-ticker drops.

The path is fully proven in code (live cron path, unconditional, not flag-gated), hence CONFIRMED rather than PLAUSIBLE. Two caveats that do not negate the finding: (1) it is latent — the finding concedes ~$0.23-0.70/cycle vs the $1.00 ceiling, so it does not explain the operator's current low-throughput (S3) symptom; it activates only when full-pipeline costs rise or analyze_top_n/the visible $2.00 knob is raised. (2) I could not read backend/.env (permission denied) to rule out a higher PYFINAGENT_SESSION_BUDGET_USD override, but the finding targets the code default, which is verified, and the silent-breach + no-log + intent-divergence defects hold at any value. Because it is a robustness/hidden-config footgun with a silent failure mode that is latent today (not currently suppressing throughput or all trades), P2 is the correct severity — it would rise to P1 only once the $1.00 ceiling becomes binding.

confirmed.title: Price-tolerance gate (5%, enabled) silently rejects volatile-name BUYs across an up-to-2h cycle window
confirmed.summary: execute_buy rejects a BUY (returns None, WARN-only) when the live fill price diverges from analysis-time price by more than paper_price_tolerance_pct (default 5.0, settings.py:546). The autonomous cycle budget is up to 7200s (paper_cycle_max_seconds, settings.py:32), and the screener favors high-momentum names, so a >5% move between analysis and execution is plausible and silently drops the buy.
confirmed.failure_scenario: A momentum semiconductor name is analyzed BUY at $540 early in a long full-pipeline cycle; by the time Step 7 executes (potentially 30-120 min later on the claude-code rail) it has moved to $570 (+5.6%). divergence_pct 5.6 > 5.0 -> execute_buy returns None. The order silently evaporates (only a WARN in backend.log); trades_executed is not incremented and nothing surfaces on the cycle summary. Latent contributor to under-trading, especially for the volatile names this screener selects.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Surface price-tolerance rejections on the cycle summary (count + tickers) so they are auditable, and consider widening the tolerance or re-fetching/re-sizing at execution rather than dropping the order outright for otherwise-valid BUYs.

verdict.reasoning: The code path is fully verified. The price-tolerance gate is real and default-armed: paper_price_tolerance_pct=5.0 (settings.py:546, ge=0 le=50, non-zero => active). At paper_trader.py:172-188 execute_buy computes divergence_pct=abs(price-price_at_analysis)/price_at_analysis*100 and, when >tolerance with price_at_analysis not None and >0, emits only logger.warning() and returns None — no BQ write, no trade record. The live FULL-pipeline path reaches this gate with a populated reference: autonomous_loop.py:1284-1318 (Step 7 buys) sets price=_get_live_price(ticker) as the fill and passes price_at_analysis=order.price_at_analysis (line 1311), which traces to portfolio_manager.py:439 <- :271 <- autonomous_loop.py:1700 (quant yf_data valuation currentPrice). Operator has Lite mode OFF, so this reference IS populated (lite path sets it None at :1862 => fail-open). The rejection is not surfaced on the cycle summary — the only buy counter is trades_executed incremented on success (:1320), and no price-tolerance counter exists anywhere in the loop; the summary (:1412) reports trades_executed only. The 2h window (paper_cycle_max_seconds=7200, settings.py:32) with all-analysis-then-all-execute ordering makes the analysis->execution price gap real. Two adversarial caveats keep this at P2 rather than higher: (1) the retained backend.log shows 0 occurrences of the reject string, and since the reject writes nothing to BQ the log is the only trace — so this is latent/occasional, NOT the current driver of the 2-position under-trading (memory attributes that to all-neutral alt-strategy labels). (2) Rejecting a BUY whose live price is 5%+ above the analysis price is a partly-intended risk control (don't chase), so the genuine defect is the SILENT/unauditable dropping of screener-favored volatile-momentum names with no operator-visible surfacing — a real robustness/observability gap (maps to S3), which the finding honestly frames as a 'latent contributor.' P2 (correctness/robustness) is the correct severity: not P1 (no empirical firings, gate is intended behavior, not a systematic suppressor), not P3 (silent drop of exactly the names the screener selects, unauditable to an operator asking 'why so few trades', is materially worth fixing via summary surfacing).

confirmed.title: 'Unknown' sector fallthrough conflates all unresolved-sector candidates and positions into one bucket that shares the count cap (2)
confirmed.summary: When sector enrichment fails, candidates get sector 'Unknown' (:272) and legacy/enrichment-miss positions also seed sector_counts under 'Unknown' (:319). The count cap at :360-369 then treats all Unknown-sector names as one sector, so two Unknown positions block every further Unknown-sector BUY (queued for a swap path that is currently effectively disabled by the churn fix).
confirmed.failure_scenario: International/newly-listed tickers (e.g., 000660.KS, 009150.KS) or yfinance rate-limited lookups resolve to sector 'Unknown'. Two such positions -> sector_counts['Unknown']=2 -> max_per_sector reached -> every subsequent candidate whose sector didn't resolve is sector-blocked at :362 and never bought, regardless of its true sector. Silently suppresses diversification/throughput whenever _fetch_ticker_meta enrichment misses.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Do not apply the count cap to the 'Unknown' bucket (skip the cap when cand_sector=='Unknown'), or fail the enrichment loudly and retry, so unresolved-sector candidates are not gated against each other as if they shared a sector.

verdict.reasoning:
The code MECHANISM is exactly as described and verified in portfolio_manager.py: line 272 gives unresolved candidates sector "Unknown"; line 319 seeds sector_counts["Unknown"] for empty-sector held positions; lines 360-362 apply the count cap to the "Unknown" bucket like any other sector, appending blocked candidates to sector_blocked (line 368) and continuing. So distinct-but-unresolved sectors do share one cap bucket. That part is code-true and not refutable.

However the FAILURE SCENARIO is not proven to occur in the live loop, and its impact is overstated:
(1) Live state: paper_positions holds exactly ONE row — AMD, sector 'Technology' (queried financial_reports.paper_positions). sector_counts = {"Technology": 1}; the "Unknown" bucket is empty and blocking nothing. The finding's precondition (two held positions BOTH resolving to "Unknown" so sector_counts["Unknown"]=2) is not met.
(2) Sector is enriched TWICE — candidate-time (autonomous_loop.py:744-761) and position-time (autonomous_loop.py:1190-1226), each BQ-first + parallel-yfinance fallback (paper_trading.py:1058-1171) — so two simultaneous Unknown *holds* are uncommon; yfinance resolves sectors for most liquid US/EU/KR tickers.
(3) Even when the Unknown bucket is at cap, only OTHER Unknown-sector candidates are blocked; resolved-sector candidates pass the count cap untouched, so cross-sector diversification is NOT suppressed.
(4) The finding claims it fires "whenever _fetch_ticker_meta enrichment misses" — false. A single Unknown miss with <2 Unknown holds blocks nothing; it requires the Unknown bucket already at 2, a narrow precondition.

Symptom linkage: this does NOT explain S2. The lone holding is Technology, not Unknown, so the mechanism is inert in the current live state. The operator's poor diversification is better explained by other factors (single position, alt strategies producing all-neutral labels -> few BUY candidates, ~66% cash).

Design-intent counterpoint: the documented concern (autonomous_loop.py:1193 comment) is the OPPOSITE direction — "Unknown" historically UNDER-enforced the cap, prompting the enrichment. Conservatively capping concentration in unclassifiable names is defensible risk management, so calling this a clear "defect" is debatable.

Verdict PLAUSIBLE (mechanism verified in code, but harmful firing is conditional on an uncommon precondition absent from the live state and thus not fully proven). real=true as a minor robustness corner (the Unknown sentinel doing double duty as both a real bucket and a fallback is a genuine code smell), but severity is overstated: it is a latent P3 robustness nit, not a P2, and it is not the cause of the reported S2 diversification symptom.

confirmed.title: Active BUY-gating and throughput knobs are absent from the settings API surface (no read/write/UI wiring)
confirmed.summary: paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct (default 30, actively blocks buys), paper_max_factor_corr, paper_swap_min_delta_pct (25) and paper_swap_max_per_cycle (2) exist as live trading gates but are missing from FullSettings, SettingsUpdate, and _FIELD_TO_ENV, so the operator cannot view or change them via the Trading-settings page.
confirmed.failure_scenario: paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct defaults to 30.0 (NOT disabled) and actively blocks a BUY whenever the projected sector NAV exceeds 30% (portfolio_manager.py:394-405). The swap-throttle knobs paper_swap_min_delta_pct=25 and paper_swap_max_per_cycle=2 (settings.py:332,338; enforced portfolio_manager.py:523-524) directly bound how many sell-to-buy-better swaps fire per cycle, which caps trade throughput (S3). grep confirms none of these four fields appear in settings_api.py (_FIELD_TO_ENV lines 257-308, SettingsUpdate 125-170, FullSettings 61-122) and none are rendered in frontend/src/app/paper-trading/manage/page.tsx (only the COUNT cap paper_max_per_sector is exposed). Consequence: to relax the hidden 30%-NAV diversification gate or loosen swap throttling to raise throughput, the operator must hand-edit backend/.env and restart uvicorn — there is no UI/API lever. This is the 'backend field with no UI wiring' completeness gap; it leaves the operator's stated S2/S3 tuning levers inaccessible from the Trading-settings surface they are using.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct, paper_max_factor_corr, paper_swap_min_delta_pct, paper_swap_max_per_cycle to FullSettings (read), SettingsUpdate (with ge/le matching settings.py bounds), and _FIELD_TO_ENV, then add PaperSettingNum rows in manage/page.tsx. Mirror the existing paper_max_per_sector wiring pattern.

verdict.reasoning:
The core wiring gap is code-verified and real: paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct (settings.py:277), paper_max_factor_corr (:291), paper_swap_min_delta_pct (:332), paper_swap_max_per_cycle (:338) all exist as backend fields but are absent from FullSettings (settings_api.py:61-122), SettingsUpdate (:125-170), and _FIELD_TO_ENV (:294-308), and are not rendered in frontend/.../manage/page.tsx (only paper_max_positions + paper_max_per_sector at :237-238). NAV cap enforcement is live (portfolio_manager.py:394-408) and swap knobs are live (:523-524, swap enabled by default). So "cannot view/change via the Trading-settings page" is TRUE.

However the finding is downgraded from P2 to P3 because of material overstatements in its failure_scenario and because it is a completeness/polish gap, not a correctness bug: (1) "there is no UI/API lever ... must hand-edit .env and restart" is FALSE for the highest-impact field — paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct IS in risk_overrides.ALLOWED_KEYS/BOUNDS (risk_overrides.py:53) and IS runtime-tunable via POST /api/paper-trading/risk-limits (paper_trading.py:596), read at decide-time via get_effective (portfolio_manager.py:306-311) with NO restart; only the swap knobs + factor_corr (read directly from settings at :312/:523/:524, not in ALLOWED_KEYS) actually require .env+restart. (2) paper_max_factor_corr defaults to 0.0 = disabled and short-circuits (:413), so calling it an active buy-blocking gate is wrong. (3) The enforcement is correct — the settings still take effect (not P0), and the conservative swap defaults + quiet-log factor_corr flag are documented intentional safety choices (settings.py:325-346), so their omission from the settings page is defensible by design, mirroring why kill-switch limits are deliberately kept out of risk_overrides. (4) As an explainer of the operator's symptoms it is weak: with 2 positions and ~66% cash each position is ~15% NAV so the 30% cap rarely binds (S2), and swaps are a secondary trade path behind the main BUY gate (S3). Net: genuine missing-UI-wiring completeness gap for advanced/experimental knobs, but enforcement works and the setting has effect — polish-tier, and the "no lever" claim is refuted for the one field that actually blocks buys.

confirmed.title: 'Starting capital' shown on the Manage page never reflects deposits (settings-config vs BQ-portfolio divergence)
confirmed.summary: FullSettings.paper_starting_capital is read from the static settings/.env value (default 10000), but the deposit endpoint mutates starting_capital only on the BigQuery paper_portfolio row, so the Manage page's 'Starting capital' ReadOnlyField stays frozen at the config default after any top-up.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Operator uses Top up fund to deposit $5,000. deposit_funds (paper_trading.py:1249) upserts paper_portfolio.starting_capital 10000 -> 15000 in BigQuery and invalidates only 'paper:*' cache keys; it never writes PAPER_STARTING_CAPITAL to .env nor touches the settings surface. The Manage page 'Starting capital' field (manage/page.tsx:201) is fed by FullSettings.paper_starting_capital, which _settings_to_full derives from settings.paper_starting_capital (settings_api.py:362) — the config default 10000. So the field keeps displaying $10,000 while the real P&L basis is $15,000. Purely a display/trust divergence between the two surfaces, but a genuine one: the number the operator reads as their cost basis is stale/wrong after any deposit.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Source the displayed starting capital from the live BQ paper_portfolio row (as the Paper Trading status/portfolio endpoints already do) rather than from settings, or drop paper_starting_capital from FullSettings and render it from the portfolio payload the Manage page already fetches.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is real and CURRENTLY MANIFESTING on the live system. I traced the full path and confirmed every link:

1. Manage page source: frontend/src/app/paper-trading/manage/page.tsx:44 loads `manageSettings` via `getFullSettings()` (GET /api/settings/, returns FullSettings). Line 199-203 renders the "Starting capital" ReadOnlyField from `manageSettings.paper_starting_capital` with hint "Adjust via Top up fund (deposit only)."

2. Settings source: settings_api.py:383-393 `get_all_settings` returns `_settings_to_full(settings)`; line 362 sets `paper_starting_capital=float(getattr(s, "paper_starting_capital", 10000.0))` — read from the Settings config object (settings.py:264 default 10000.0, backed by PAPER_STARTING_CAPITAL in .env). Confirmed it is NEVER derived from the BQ portfolio row.

3. Deposit endpoint: paper_trading.py:1219-1272 `deposit_funds` reads the BQ portfolio, computes `new_starting = starting_before + amount` (line 1238), upserts BQ `paper_portfolio.starting_capital` (line 1254), and invalidates ONLY `paper:*` cache keys (line 1255). It does NOT write PAPER_STARTING_CAPITAL to .env, does NOT touch the settings surface, and does NOT invalidate the `settings:full` cache. Confirmed the two surfaces diverge after any deposit. The frontend `handleDeposit` (page.tsx:58-84) refreshes status/portfolio but never re-fetches getFullSettings nor updates manageSettings, so the field is stale even in-session and after reload.

4. Live grounding: I queried BQ `financial_reports.paper_portfolio` — `starting_capital = 20000.0` (a $10k deposit was made; 10000 -> 20000) while `settings.paper_starting_capital = 10000.0`. So the Manage page's "Starting capital" field displays $10,000 RIGHT NOW while the real cost basis is $20,000 — a live, active 2x divergence, not merely latent.

The failure scenario is exactly as described. This is a display/trust divergence only: the actual P&L math uses the BQ starting_capital (deposit computes new_pnl_pct from new_starting; status/portfolio endpoints read BQ at lines 99/152/335), so no money path or trade decision is affected. Severity stays P3 — it is a read-only informational field with zero downstream functional consequence — though the fact that the field is currently wrong and the UI hint ("Adjust via Top up fund") actively promises behavior the code doesn't deliver makes it a genuine (not cosmetic) correctness/trust bug at the P2/P3 boundary. It is unrelated to symptoms S1/S2/S3 (general). Fix sketch in the finding is valid: source the displayed value from the BQ paper_portfolio row (already available in the portfolio/status payloads) instead of settings.

confirmed.title: Swap path fires on a fabricated 0.0-conviction sentinel (churn engine) — churn-fix flag is default OFF and LIVE
confirmed.summary: With paper_swap_churn_fix_enabled default OFF (settings.py:344), a sector-blocked candidate is compared against the weakest same-sector holding, but a holding with no same-cycle re-eval is assigned final_score=0.0 (line 562-565) and the swap-delta denominator uses a 0.01 epsilon (line 620), so delta_pct explodes to ~tens-of-thousands of percent and ANY candidate clears the 25% bar — the holding is swapped out regardless of true relative conviction.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Re-eval is gated to every paper_reeval_frequency_days (~3 days, autonomous_loop:875), so on most cycles a held position has NO entry in holding_lookup. When its sector reaches paper_max_per_sector (2) and a new candidate is blocked (line 359-368), _compute_swap_candidates sets the holding score to sentinel 0.0 (line 562-565); denom=max(0,0.01)=0.01 (line 620); delta_pct=(cand_score-0)/0.01*100 ≈ 70,000% >> 25% min_delta → swap fires (paper_swap_enabled default True, settings.py:328). This is the documented AW-5 churn engine: MU/SNDK/DELL round trips, 81.4% weekly turnover, net -$132. It generates many value-destroying trades (the wrong kind of throughput) and keeps the book pinned in one sector. Not the current-state trigger (only 1 position now, no sector at cap) but armed the moment the book concentrates.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Set paper_swap_churn_fix_enabled=True (operator-gated per live_check_60.2.md). The ON path already (a) excludes no-same-cycle-analysis holdings from displacement and (b) clamps the denominator to max(abs(score),1.0). This is the intended fix sitting behind a default-OFF flag.

verdict.reasoning: Verified the full call path in code; the defect is real and even self-labeled "KNOWN DEFECT (AW-5)" in the source. With live defaults (paper_swap_enabled=True settings.py:328; paper_swap_churn_fix_enabled=False settings.py:344; max_per_sector=2; paper_swap_max_per_cycle=2 so _compute_swap_candidates does not short-circuit at portfolio_manager.py:525; min_delta=25 settings.py:332), a sector-blocked candidate (captured at portfolio_manager.py:359-369) is compared against the weakest same-sector holding. Re-eval is gated every 3 days (paper_reeval_frequency_days default 3, settings.py:369; autonomous_loop.py:871-876), so reeval_tickers -> holding_analyses -> holding_lookup (portfolio_manager.py:99-103) OMITS any holding re-evaluated within the last 3 days. On between-reeval cycles that holding is absent from holding_lookup: _compute_swap_candidates does holding_lookup.get(ticker,{})={}, final_score=None, and the flag-OFF branch assigns sentinel score=0.0 (portfolio_manager.py:537-566). denom then = max(abs(0.0),0.01)=0.01 (line 620) and delta_pct=(cand_score-0)/0.01*100 = cand_score*10000 (line 621), which for any positive-conviction blocked candidate (final_score from portfolio_manager.py:211/270/593) is thousands of percent, trivially exceeding the 25% bar at line 623 -> swap SELL+BUY fires regardless of true relative conviction. holding_lookup is built only from holding_analyses (never candidate_analyses), so the sentinel is unavoidable on between-reeval cycles. This is the documented AW-5 churn engine (MU/SNDK/DELL round trips, 81.4% weekly turnover, net -$132), historically observed live, not merely hypothetical. Every file:line citation in the finding checks out. The only reason it is not firing at this instant is the precondition that a sector be at the count cap (needs >=2 same-sector holdings plus a 3rd blocked candidate); with ~2 total positions today it is dormant, exactly as the finding states ("armed the moment the book concentrates") -- latent-but-real, so CONFIRMED rather than REFUTED. Severity P1 is correct: not P0 (does not universally break the money path or drop all trades; requires concentration precondition and generates trades rather than zeroing them), and more than P2 (documented material P&L loss, worsens S2 diversification by pinning the book in one sector, and manufactures value-destroying churn = the wrong kind of S3 throughput). The intended fix already sits behind the operator-gated paper_swap_churn_fix_enabled flag (clamps denom to max(abs(score),1.0) and excludes non-re-eval'd holdings from displacement).

confirmed.title: "Unknown" sector bucket conflates all unsectored candidates into a single 2-count / 30%-NAV cap → suppresses cross-sector buys
confirmed.summary: Candidate sector resolves to the literal string "Unknown" whenever enrichment does not supply one (line 221-227, 272). Both the count cap (line 360-368) and the NAV-pct cap (line 394-408) key on that string, so ALL candidates that lack a resolved sector are treated as the SAME sector and share one cap of 2 (and one 30%-of-NAV ceiling) — even when their true GICS sectors are all different.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Candidate sectors come ONLY from the best-effort _fetch_ticker_meta enrichment (autonomous_loop:744-761), which is wrapped in a fail-open try/except (the screener does not attach sectors unless sector_neutral/multidim flags are on, line 432-438, both default OFF). On any partial yfinance/BQ failure, N candidates in N distinct real sectors (e.g. Energy, Healthcare, Financials) all collapse to "Unknown"; after 2 buys the 3rd+ is sector-blocked (line 362) despite being a genuinely new sector — directly the 'no buys from other sectors' symptom. If held positions are also unsectored (legacy rows; enrichment at :1198-1226 is likewise fail-open), the Unknown bucket is pre-seeded and blocks even sooner. The NAV-pct cap compounds it: once unsectored holdings exceed 30% of NAV they block every other Unknown candidate.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Do not fold missing-sector candidates into one shared cap. Either (a) exempt sector="Unknown"/empty from the count+NAV caps (fail-open — an unknown sector should not consume a real sector's slot), or (b) make sector resolution mandatory/hard-fail before a candidate can be capped, or (c) bucket unknowns per-ticker so they don't collide. Also make the enrichment failure loud (it is currently a silent warning).

verdict.reasoning:
The core code defect is REAL and verified. portfolio_manager.py folds every unresolved-sector candidate into the literal string "Unknown" (line 272, re-derived at :360 and :456) and buckets held positions with empty sector the same way (:319). Both the count cap (:360-368, keyed on `cand.get("sector") or "Unknown"`) and the NAV-pct cap (:394-408) key on that single string with no exemption, so N candidates with unresolved sectors share ONE cap of `paper_max_per_sector` (default 2) and one 30%-NAV ceiling regardless of their true GICS sectors. The path is live-reachable: `paper_max_per_sector` defaults to 2 (>0) so the block runs every cycle (settings.py:269), and the ONLY live source of candidate sector is the fail-open `_fetch_ticker_meta` (autonomous_loop.py:744-761) — the screener's sector attach (screener.py:233 `if sector_lookup:`) is gated on sector_neutral_momentum_enabled / multidim_momentum_enabled, both default False (settings.py:427,437), confirmed. So a candidate with unresolved sector WILL be conflated into the shared Unknown cap.

However, the finding's failure_scenario is overstated and I could not confirm it as the live cause of S2. (1) The yfinance fallback is PER-TICKER fail-open (paper_trading.py:1161-1167 catches each worker and continues), NOT all-or-nothing — a single ticker failure makes only that ticker Unknown; a broad collapse needs BOTH the BQ query failing AND yfinance failing across many tickers at once, not "any partial failure." (2) BQ grounding: the current book is one position (AMD) carrying a RESOLVED sector "Technology", so the Unknown bucket currently starts empty (no legacy unsectored rows pre-seeding the NAV cap), and recent BUYs (AMD, MU, SNDK, STX, DELL, HPE, Samsung, SK Hynix) are all tech/semis — the pool is tech-concentrated, so S2 is more plausibly driven by the REAL Technology cap of 2 firing on resolved sectors than by Unknown conflation. (3) The count cap only bites at the 3rd+ simultaneously-unsectored candidate; the first two unsectored buys clear. Real latent risk remains (67% of recent analysis_results have empty/NULL sector — 278/416; EU/KR tickers have spottier yfinance coverage; a yfinance rate-limit event would collapse many candidates to Unknown), so it is a genuine fail-open-enrichment-feeds-fail-closed-cap robustness bug. That is a correctness/robustness defect that suppresses diversification only under enrichment failure, not in the common case — P2, not P1. real=true, verdict PLAUSIBLE because the code defect is confirmed but its causal link to the live S2 symptom is unproven and the finding overstates the trigger.

confirmed.title: Swap BUY is not bounded by available_cash and skips the $50 floor → non-atomic pair can execute the SELL but silently drop the BUY (net position loss)
confirmed.summary: The swap BUY is sized buy_amount = nav * position_pct/100 (line 675) with NO min(available_cash) cap and NO <$50 skip — unlike the main buy loop (line 381-389). The swap pair is emitted as SELL+BUY but executed non-atomically: all SELLs run first, then BUYs (autonomous_loop:1262-1318), and execute_buy returns None if total_cost > cash (paper_trader.py:197-199).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Well-invested book, low cash: the main buy loop consumes available_cash on its own buys (leaving ~min_cash = 5% NAV). The swap path then appends up to paper_swap_max_per_cycle=2 uncapped BUYs at ~nav*10% each = ~20% NAV, plus their SELLs. At execution the swap SELLs fire unconditionally (weak holdings closed, cash freed only by their small market_value), but the summed BUY notional exceeds cash, so execute_buy hits total_cost>cash and returns None for the 2nd swap BUY → it is silently dropped. Result: sold the holding, did NOT buy the replacement → position count drops by 1 and the sector is left with a hole — the exact opposite of the swap's +1/-1 net-zero intent.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Bound the swap buy_amount by a running available_cash tracker shared with the main loop and apply the same <$50 skip; or make the swap pair truly atomic (roll back / skip the SELL if the paired BUY cannot be funded).

verdict.reasoning: Every code fact in the finding is verified. (1) portfolio_manager.py:675 sizes the swap BUY as `buy_amount = nav * (float(position_pct)/100.0)` with NO min(available_cash) cap and NO <$50 skip, unlike the main loop (line 381 `min(target_amount, available_cash)` + line 384 `if buy_amount < 50: continue`). (2) The swap path `_compute_swap_candidates` (call at 471-480) is never passed `available_cash`, so the main loop's running cash tracker (decremented at line 450) does not constrain swap buys. (3) Execution is non-atomic: autonomous_loop.py runs ALL SELLs (1262-1275) then ALL BUYs (1284-1320), with orders.sort at 491 forcing SELLs-first and swap BUYs appended last (481). (4) paper_trader.py:197-199 returns None when total_cost > cash (logs a warning, no rollback of the already-executed paired SELL). (5) The path is default-live: settings.py defaults paper_swap_enabled=True (329), paper_swap_max_per_cycle=2 (339), paper_max_per_sector=2 (269) — no .env override required. There is no aggregate cash validation on the final orders list before execution. Concrete failing case: NAV $100k, min_cash 5% ($5k), start cash $8k, no step-1 sells; two sectors at the count cap each yield a blocked strong candidate clearing the 25% delta; Swap1 SELLs weak $5k Tech → BUY new Tech $10k, Swap2 SELLs weak $5k HC → BUY new HC $10k. Cash after all SELLs = $18k, total swap BUY notional = $20k > $18k, so the last swap BUY returns None while its paired SELL already fired → net position count -1 and a sector hole — the exact inversion of the +1/-1 intent. Caveat justifying P2 (not P1): the failure requires a well-invested, low-cash book (cash near the 5% reserve) with >=2 cross-sector blocked candidates each clearing the delta bar. The current live book (2 positions, ~66% cash) funds swaps easily, so this is a LATENT robustness/correctness bug and does NOT explain the current S2/S3 symptoms. The word 'silently' is slightly imprecise (execute_buy emits a WARNING at line 198) but the substantive claim — no rollback, net position loss — is correct. Verdict CONFIRMED because the drop mechanism and code path are fully proven in code and reachable under defaults; only the specific runtime portfolio state (not the code path) is unobserved. Corrected severity P2.

confirmed.title: Add-to-existing-position corrupts avg_entry_price for non-US tickers (USD cost / local-share unit mix)
confirmed.summary: On a follow-on BUY of an existing position, new_cost = old_cost + amount_usd (USD, line 307) and new_avg = new_cost/new_qty (line 308), where new_qty is in LOCAL shares (quantity = amount_usd*_usd_to_local/price, line 217). The result is stored as avg_entry_price (line 314), which the rest of the engine treats as a LOCAL-currency price (e.g. realized_pnl_pct = (local_price - avg_entry_price)/avg_entry_price, execute_sell:423; mark_to_market pnl, and the stop-loss comparison current<=stop).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Buy 005930.KS (KRW) once (avg_entry_price = local KRW price, correct), then buy more in a later cycle → new_avg becomes USD-per-local-share (mixes USD numerator with KRW-share denominator). Subsequent realized_pnl_pct = (KRW price ~70000 - USD avg ~50)/USD avg → a nonsense +100,000% gain, or the inverse a near -100% loss, which can trip the stop-loss (current<=stop) and force a spurious exit, and poisons round-trip P&L reporting. First buy is fine; only ADD-ONs corrupt it. Low live impact today (book is US-only, where _usd_to_local=1.0 so USD==local), but real once EU/KR positions are added to.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Convert amount_usd to local currency before folding into the local-denominated cost basis: new_local_cost = old_qty*avg_entry_price + amount_usd*_usd_to_local; new_avg = new_local_cost/new_qty. Keep avg_entry_price strictly in the position's local currency (consistent with the first-buy path and current_price).

verdict.reasoning:
The core unit-mix defect is verified directly in code. On a follow-on BUY of an existing position, paper_trader.py:307 computes new_cost = old_cost + amount_usd (both USD, since cost_basis is stored USD per base_currency="USD" at lines 315/330/339/351), then line 308 divides by new_qty which is in LOCAL shares (quantity = amount_usd*_usd_to_local/price, line 217), and line 314 stores that USD-per-local-share value as avg_entry_price. Everywhere else avg_entry_price is a LOCAL-currency price: the first-buy path stores avg_entry_price = price (local) at line 338, current_price is local (line 316), and execute_sell computes realized_pnl_pct = (price - avg_entry_price)/avg_entry_price at lines 422-424 with price = local sell price. For a KRW position the add-on avg becomes ~local_price*_local_to_usd (e.g. 70000/1300 ≈ 53.8 vs true 70000), so realized_pnl_pct explodes to ~+130,000% and realized_pnl_usd (lines 472-473) plus the round-trip rows are poisoned. For US, _fx_usd_to_local and _fx_local_to_usd both return 1.0 (lines 39-40, 61-62), so US positions are byte-identical/correct — the bug is strictly non-US, matching the finding. The average-up unit test (test_dod4_tier1_coverage_investment.py:594) only exercises AAPL (US, fx=1.0), so the non-US case is genuinely unhandled. The fix_sketch (new_local_cost = old_qty*avg_entry_price + amount_usd*_usd_to_local; new_avg = new_local_cost/new_qty) is arithmetically correct and restores the local price.

Two secondary claims in the finding are overstated and I correct them: (1) mark_to_market P&L is NOT poisoned — it derives pnl from cost_basis (USD, correctly maintained at line 315) and market_value (USD), never avg_entry_price (lines 553-555); unrealized P&L and NAV are unaffected. (2) The stop-loss "spurious exit" mechanism is imprecise — stop_loss_price is a separate stored column, not recomputed from avg_entry_price on the add-on branch; it is only derivable from the corrupted avg via backfill_missing_stops (line 793), which fires only when the stop is None, and for KRW the corrupted-DOWN avg would make the stop uselessly low (never fires), the opposite of a spurious exit. The actual cash credit on sell uses net_proceeds*_l2u (line 517), independent of avg_entry_price, so cash accounting stays correct.

Reachability is doubly latent, which the finding partly concedes: the primary autonomous loop's decide_trades SKIPS already-held tickers (portfolio_manager.py:180), so the normal live cycle never averages up — the exact "buy more in a later cycle" trigger as written is blocked on the main path. The add-on branch is reachable via the signals_server MCP publish path (signals_server.py:411, which does not apply the held-ticker filter) and crash/retry edges, and the book is US-only today (_fx=1.0). Verdict CONFIRMED because the code defect and its primary downstream consumer (execute_sell realized_pnl_pct) are verified line-by-line; severity P2 is correct — a real correctness/robustness defect confined to realized-P&L reporting for non-US add-ons, not a live money-path corruption (cash/NAV/unrealized P&L all remain correct), and currently dormant.

confirmed.title: NAV%-per-sector cap has no settings API field or UI editor; source comment falsely claims it is editable at /manage
confirmed.summary: paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct (default 30% NAV/sector) is a live backend diversification cap enforced in portfolio_manager.py:308/645 and autonomous_loop.py:1886, but it is completely absent from the settings API (not in settings_api.py's FullSettings/SettingsUpdate/_FIELD_TO_ENV/_settings_to_full), absent from frontend types.ts, and has NO input in the Trading settings form; the sector-bar cap is hardcoded to the constant DEFAULT_SECTOR_CAP_PCT=30 and a source comment lies that the setting 'lives at /paper-trading/manage'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Operator opens Paper Trading > Settings (/paper-trading/manage) intending to change the per-sector cap. Only 'Max positions per sector' (the COUNT cap paper_max_per_sector) exists. The other active per-sector cap (paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct, 30% of NAV per GICS sector, settings.py:277) has no control anywhere in the UI, and no field in GET/PUT /api/settings/, so the operator cannot change it from the app at all (only by hand-editing .env or the risk_overrides audit file). Meanwhile the Sector concentration bar on /paper-trading/positions always renders 'Cap: 30% per sector' regardless of the true configured value because capPct is the module constant DEFAULT_SECTOR_CAP_PCT (positions/page.tsx:29,158), so even if the value were changed via .env the UI would keep showing 30. The comment at positions/page.tsx:24 asserting it is reachable/updatable via /manage is factually wrong.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Add paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct to backend SettingsUpdate + FullSettings + _FIELD_TO_ENV + _settings_to_full (mirroring paper_max_per_sector), add it to frontend types.ts FullSettings, add a PaperSettingNum (min 0 max 100 step 5) in manage/page.tsx, then fetch FullSettings on the positions page (or via the shared paper-trading context) and pass the real value to SectorBarList capPct instead of the hardcoded constant. Delete the false comment.

verdict.reasoning: Every claim verified in code. (1) paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct is a real, live backend cap: settings.py:277 (default 30.0, ge=0 le=100), enforced unconditionally in portfolio_manager.py:308/645 via risk_overrides.get_effective. (2) It is absent from settings_api.py -- `grep nav_pct` returns nothing; only the COUNT cap paper_max_per_sector is in FullSettings(103)/SettingsUpdate(157)/_FIELD_TO_ENV(296)/_settings_to_full(364). So GET/PUT /api/settings/ cannot read or write it. (3) Absent from frontend/src except two code comments (positions/page.tsx:24, SectorBarList.tsx:27); no types.ts field, no form input. (4) manage/page.tsx:238 has a PaperSettingNum for the count cap paper_max_per_sector but nothing for the nav_pct cap -- confirming the operator cannot change it in-app. (5) The sector-bar cap is the module constant DEFAULT_SECTOR_CAP_PCT=30 (positions/page.tsx:29) passed to SectorBarList capPct (:158); SectorBarList.tsx:103 renders "Cap: {capPct}% per sector" and capPct also drives the amber/rose concentration color bands (bandFor, lines 36-38), so the risk view thresholds against 30 regardless of the true value. (6) The comment at positions/page.tsx:23-28 is false on two counts: it claims the setting "lives at /paper-trading/manage" (grep of manage/page.tsx confirms it does not) and that the cap "will update when /manage refetches" (structurally impossible -- it is a module constant with no fetch/state wiring). The failure scenario is reached: with 2 positions held the positions page unconditionally renders the hardcoded cap and its warning bands. I was tasked to refute, but the code clearly supports the defect, so I cannot. Severity P2 is correct: not P0/P1 because the backend cap functions at its default (no trades dropped, 30% is generous and not diversification-suppressing), and above P3 because the source comment asserts editability and auto-update behavior that do not exist and any .env/risk_overrides change would make the risk-monitoring UI silently misreport the concentration threshold. Note: this is tangential to the operator's S1 (which concerns the COUNT cap paper_max_per_sector -- that one DOES have a UI editor at manage:238); it maps to the "general" dimension.

confirmed.title: Number settings inputs cannot be cleared: emptying the field snaps it back to the stored value
confirmed.summary: PaperSettingNum computes value = draft ?? stored ?? '' and its onChange deletes the dirty entry whenever the raw input is empty (raw === '' -> next undefined -> delete merged[field]); because the controlled input's fallback (stored) is non-empty, clearing the field re-renders it back to the old number, so a user who clears-then-types sees the setting refuse to blank out.
confirmed.failure_scenario: On /paper-trading/manage the 'Max positions per sector' input shows 2 (or 'Max simultaneous positions' shows 20). Operator triple-clicks to select all and presses Backspace/Delete to clear the box before typing a new number. onChange fires with raw='' -> next=undefined -> setDirty deletes the field (cockpit-helpers.tsx:479-483) -> re-render computes value = undefined ?? 2 ?? '' = 2 -> React overwrites the now-empty DOM input back to '2'. The field visibly repopulates with the old value, making it feel like the setting 'won't change'. (Replace-typing over the selection still works, so it is friction, not a hard block — hence P2 — but it exactly matches the 'I can't change it' report.)
confirmed.fix_sketch: Represent the cleared state explicitly instead of deleting on empty: keep the field in dirty as '' (or a sentinel) while empty and only prune it on blur / when it equals stored, or store the raw string in local state and coerce to number only at save time so the input can legitimately be empty mid-edit.

verdict.reasoning: Verified in code end-to-end. PaperSettingNum (frontend/src/components/paper-trading/cockpit-helpers.tsx:444-498) renders a controlled <input type="number" value={value}> with value = (draft ?? stored ?? "") at line 467. It is used live for BOTH S1 settings on /paper-trading/manage (page.tsx:237-238: paper_max_positions, paper_max_per_sector) with no feature flag and no disabled/readOnly attribute, so the path is reached. State: manageSettings from getFullSettings() gives stored=2 (default/live value, non-empty); manageDirty starts {} so draft=undefined and value resolves to 2. On clear, onChange fires raw==="" -> next=undefined -> setDirty deletes the field (lines 479-483, a no-op since it was never set) -> re-render recomputes value = undefined ?? 2 = 2 -> React's controlled-input reconciliation writes the DOM node's .value back to "2" (value prop unchanged at 2 while DOM was ""), so the emptied field snaps back to the old number. This is the canonical 'controlled number input refuses to clear' React behavior and is deterministic; the only unverifiable step is a live browser render (Playwright reserved for the main agent), but the code path is fully proven. Severity P2 is correct, not P0/P1: the setting IS still changeable via select-all-then-replace-typing (raw='5' -> next=5 != 2 -> dirty set, 'unsaved' badge shows) and handleSettingsSave persists manageDirty via updateSettings (page.tsx:95). So it is genuine edit friction that matches the operator's 'I can't change it' report as a contributing factor, but not a hard block and not 'setting has no effect' (that would be a separate backend/scheduler cause). The finding's own P2 self-assessment and rationale are accurate.

confirmed.title: Out-of-range numeric settings are accepted client-side and fail only at save with a generic 422 banner
confirmed.summary: PaperSettingNum's HTML min/max are advisory only; onChange stores any typed number into dirty without clamping, so an out-of-range value passes silently until Save, where the backend Pydantic Field bound rejects it with 422 surfaced as a vague 'Invalid request to /api/settings/' banner with no field-level indication.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Operator types 99 into 'Max simultaneous positions' (backend SettingsUpdate caps it at le=50, settings_api.py:156). The input accepts 99 (max=50 only styles validity, does not block typing), Save is enabled, updateSettings PUTs {paper_max_positions:99}, backend returns HTTP 422, apiFetch (api.ts:125-127) throws 'Invalid request to /api/settings/: ...', shown as a rose banner that does not name the field or the allowed range. The operator concludes the setting is broken rather than out of range.
confirmed.fix_sketch: Clamp/validate on blur before adding to dirty (and/or disable Save when any dirty field is outside [min,max]) and show a per-field inline validation message; parse the backend 422 detail to point at the offending field.

verdict.reasoning: Every link in the failure chain is verified in code. (1) PaperSettingNum (cockpit-helpers.tsx:471-491) renders type="number" with min/max, which are advisory only — HTML5 number inputs mark out-of-range values :invalid but do NOT block typing, and no checkValidity() is called; onChange (477-489) stores Number(raw) into dirty with no clamp. (2) manage/page.tsx:237-238 wires paper_max_positions (min=1 max=50) and paper_max_per_sector (min=0 max=20). (3) The Save button (manage/page.tsx:176) is disabled only on settingsSaving or empty dirty — no range-validity gate, so an out-of-range dirty value leaves Save enabled. (4) handleSettingsSave (95) -> updateSettings (api.ts:251-255) PUTs the raw body to /api/settings/. (5) The PUT endpoint (settings_api.py:396-397) binds body: SettingsUpdate, whose paper_max_positions is Field(None, ge=1, le=50) (:156) and paper_max_per_sector is Field(None, ge=0, le=20) (:157), so 99 triggers a FastAPI 422 before the handler runs. (6) apiFetch (api.ts:125-127) on 422 throws "Invalid request to /api/settings/: ${detail}"; FastAPI's 422 detail is an array of error objects, so template coercion actually yields "[object Object]" — even vaguer than described. handleSettingsSave catch (100) -> setSettingsError -> rose banner (manage/page.tsx:186-190) shows only the message, no field name or allowed range. The finding is accurately scoped: it limits its claim to out-of-range typed values, and the backend correctly rejects them (nothing corrupt persists, .env unwritten), which is why P3 (validation-UX/error-surfacing polish) is the right severity rather than a correctness/money-path bug. Important caveat: this defect does NOT explain the operator's S1 for the realistic in-range case (changing sector cap 2->3/4/5, all within ge=0 le=20) — those saves succeed through this same path — so S1's real cause is elsewhere (stale-settings-in-scheduler / setting-has-no-effect suspects).

refuted.title: Settings PUT writes .env FILE only; os.environ shadows it, so MAX POSITIONS PER SECTOR / MAX SIMULTANEOUS POSITIONS silently do not change until a full backend restart
refuted.summary: PUT /api/settings/ persists paper_max_per_sector / paper_max_positions by writing ONLY the backend/.env file (_update_env_var, 311-325), then does get_settings.cache_clear() + re-instantiate (445-447). But pydantic-settings ranks process environment (os.environ) ABOVE the env_file (settings.py:587 model_config env_file). If the backend process was started with the .env sourced into its environment, the re-read returns the STALE os.environ value, the setting change is invisible until a process restart, and even the PUT response returns the old value.
refuted.failure_scenario: Operator (or a cron/watchdog wrapper) starts the backend after `set -a; . backend/.env` — a pattern explicitly documented in settings.py:72-74 as used by the cron wrappers, and easy to hit from an interactive shell that sourced .env before launching uvicorn. os.environ now contains PAPER_MAX_PER_SECTOR=2. Operator opens Paper Trading > Manage > Trading settings, changes 'Max positions per sector' to 5, clicks Save. handleSettingsSave -> updateSettings({paper_max_per_sector:5}) -> PUT writes PAPER_MAX_PER_SECTOR=5 to the .env FILE, calls get_settings.cache_clear(), then get_settings() re-instantiates Settings(): pydantic reads os.environ FIRST and returns the stale 2. The PUT response body (_settings_to_full(settings), settings_api.py:450) therefore contains paper_max_per_sector=2; the frontend runs setManageSettings(updated) (manage/page.tsx:96) and the input snaps back to 2. Every subsequent autonomous cycle also reads 2 (get_settings() -> stale os.environ). Net: the operator changes the setting, it reverts / has no effect, forever until a restart — exactly S1. Same mechanism applies to paper_max_positions (MAX SIMULTANEOUS POSITIONS).
refuted.fix_sketch: In the PUT handler, after writing each field to the file, ALSO update the live process env so the re-read reflects it: `os.environ[env_key] = env_value` inside the loop at settings_api.py:431-442 (and del/pop when clearing). Alternatively invert precedence so the file wins, or reload the .env into os.environ before re-instantiating. Add a verification read-back (compare returned value to requested value; log/raise on mismatch). Ensure the launchd/start scripts never `set -a; . backend/.env` before starting uvicorn so os.environ stays clean on the primary path.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding's *mechanism* is technically correct — pydantic-settings ranks os.environ ABOVE the env_file, and settings.py:587 uses only `model_config = {"env_file": ...}` with no `settings_customise_sources` override, so if PAPER_MAX_PER_SECTOR were in os.environ it WOULD shadow the file. But the finding's precondition ("the backend process was started with the .env sourced into its environment") is FALSE for this deployment, so the failure scenario cannot occur on the live path.

Decisive evidence:
1) LIVE START PATH does not source .env. The backend runs as launchd service `com.pyfinagent.backend` (~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.pyfinagent.backend.plist). Its ProgramArguments exec uvicorn DIRECTLY: `/usr/bin/caffeinate -i -s .venv/bin/uvicorn backend.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000` — no shell, no `set -a; . backend/.env`. Its `EnvironmentVariables` dict sets ONLY CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN, DEV_LOCALHOST_BYPASS, PATH, PYTHONUNBUFFERED — not PAPER_MAX_PER_SECTOR/PAPER_MAX_POSITIONS.
2) EMPIRICAL PROOF on the running process. `ps eww -p 98681` (the live uvicorn) shows `DEV_LOCALHOST_BYPASS=1` present (confirming the plist env applies) but PAPER_MAX_PER_SECTOR, PAPER_MAX_POSITIONS, and PAPER_MARKETS are ABSENT from the process environment. So pydantic-settings reads those from the .env FILE, not os.environ — there is no shadow to defeat the write.
3) No load_dotenv in the backend import path. Only backend/main.py + settings.py matter for the server; neither calls load_dotenv (the sole backend load_dotenv is in backtest/spot_checks_harness.py, an offline utility). So nothing copies .env into os.environ at import.
4) Manual start path is also clean: scripts/start_services.sh only `source "$VENV"` (venv activate) before uvicorn — it never sources backend/.env.
5) The settings.py:72-74 comment the finding cites ("cron wrappers `set -a; . backend/.env`") refers to the AUTORESEARCH/ABLATION nightly cron jobs (scripts/autoresearch/run_nightly.sh) — separate short-lived processes that do NOT serve PUT /api/settings/ and do NOT host the paper-trading HTTP loop. Misattributing that shell pattern to the backend server is the finding's core error.
6) The secondary claim ("every subsequent autonomous cycle also reads stale 2") also fails: the APScheduler callback `_scheduled_run()` (paper_trading.py:1325-1327) calls `get_settings()` FRESH each run rather than using a captured object, so after the PUT's `get_settings.cache_clear()` (settings_api.py:445) it re-instantiates Settings() and re-reads the updated .env file.

Net: PUT `/api/settings/` writes the field to backend/.env via _update_env_var (settings_api.py:442), calls get_settings.cache_clear() + re-instantiates (445-447); because os.environ has no PAPER_MAX_* key on the live process, the fresh Settings() picks up the NEW file value. The PUT response body returns the updated value and the next cycle reads it too. S1 ("can't change MAX POSITIONS PER SECTOR") is a real operator symptom but is NOT caused by this os.environ-shadow mechanism — it must be traced elsewhere (UI submit wiring / endpoint / a different code path). Corrected severity P3: this is at most a latent footgun that would only bite if the deployment were ever changed to source .env into os.environ before launch — a pattern the project explicitly avoids (backend_watchdog.sh even greps .env deliberately WITHOUT sourcing it to avoid env leakage).

refuted.title: Sector-blocked-candidate swap path only displaces SAME-sector holdings, so blocked candidates churn within the hot sector instead of rotating the book into a new sector
refuted.summary: When a candidate is blocked by the per-sector count cap it is queued in sector_blocked (portfolio_manager.py:359-369) and handed to _compute_swap_candidates, which indexes holdings by sector (:531-535) and only ever considers holdings in the candidate's OWN sector (`sector_holdings = holdings_by_sector.get(cand_sector, [])`, :594), emitting a SELL+BUY pair inside that same sector (:667-693). The swap path can therefore never move the portfolio into a different sector; it only rotates names within the already-capped sector while open position slots (max_positions=20 vs ~2 held) sit unused.
refuted.failure_scenario: AMD is held (Technology, cap=2). A third semiconductor candidate is sector-blocked and enters the swap path. The swap displaces a Technology holding for another Technology name rather than buying a Consumer/Healthcare/Financials name into an empty slot. BQ shows this exact pathology: 13 swap_buy + 13 swap_for_higher_conviction SELLs, all Tech (MU<->SNDK<->DELL<->STX round trips), plus 14 stop_loss_triggers — high intra-sector churn that keeps the book pinned to 1-2 Technology positions and never diversifies, even though 18 position slots are free.
refuted.fix_sketch: When a candidate is sector-blocked AND the portfolio has free slots (remaining_positions < max_positions), the loop should surface/buy the best OTHER-sector candidate rather than swapping within the blocked sector. Diversification should take priority over within-sector displacement while slots remain. Longer term, the sector-blocked queue should feed a cross-sector fallback selector, not only the same-sector swap.

verdict.reasoning: The finding's code description is accurate but its causal attribution to S2 is refuted by the control flow. Other-sector buying is handled by the MAIN buy loop (portfolio_manager.py:339-458), which iterates ALL buy_candidates and blocks a candidate ONLY when its OWN sector is at cap (`current_in_sector >= max_per_sector`, :362-369). A Consumer/Healthcare/Financials candidate (sector count 0 < cap 2) is bought directly into a free slot in that loop — it is never diverted to sector_blocked. The swap path runs AFTER the main loop and operates solely on the same-sector-capped residual, so it can never be "choosing" to swap Tech instead of buying an other-sector name into an empty slot: such a name is already bought upstream or was never in buy_candidates. Each swap is net-zero on position count (-1 SELL/+1 BUY same sector) and runs after all main-loop buys, so it neither consumes free slots nor starves the main loop's cash — it cannot cause the 18 idle slots. The path IS live-reachable (paper_swap_enabled default True :329, paper_swap_max_per_cycle default 2 :339, paper_max_per_sector default 2 :269), and it does produce the intra-sector churn the BQ rows show (MU/SNDK/DELL round trips) — but that is a turnover/cost behavior that INCREASES trade count, not a diversification blocker, and it already has an explicit mitigation flag paper_swap_churn_fix_enabled (:344). The true root cause of S2 is upstream Tech-heavy candidate generation (alt strategies emit ~all-neutral labels → few/no other-sector candidates), which the swap path is orthogonal to. The fix sketch is incoherent: the swap path cannot "surface the best other-sector candidate" because any such candidate is already bought by the main loop. Not P1/S2; at most a P3 churn note.

refuted.title: 'Unknown' sector bucketing: if ticker-meta enrichment fails, all candidates/positions collapse into one bucket and the count cap freezes the book at 2 positions across ALL true sectors
refuted.summary: Candidate sector defaults to 'Unknown' when empty (portfolio_manager.py:272) and existing-position sector likewise (:319-320 `(pos.get('sector') or '').strip() or 'Unknown'`); the count cap then treats 'Unknown' as a single GICS bucket (:360-362). Candidate sector comes from _fetch_ticker_meta enrichment (autonomous_loop.py:744-761) which is wrapped in a non-fatal try/except — on failure sectors stay empty and every candidate/position lumps into 'Unknown'.
refuted.failure_scenario: A yfinance/BQ ticker_meta outage makes the candidate enrichment except-swallow (only a WARN log). Every candidate then carries sector='Unknown'; with paper_max_per_sector=2 the count cap blocks every BUY after the 2nd position regardless of the names' true (diverse) sectors, hard-freezing the book at 2 and silently suppressing all further diversification. Not currently active (BQ shows AMD='Technology', so sectors are populated now) — this is a latent footgun that would produce exactly the reported 'stuck at 2 positions' symptom if enrichment degrades.
refuted.fix_sketch: Exclude 'Unknown'/empty-sector names from the count-cap bucket (don't let unclassified names share one capped bucket), or fail-open the sector cap when the share of 'Unknown' candidates exceeds a threshold, and make enrichment failure loud (alert/retry) rather than a silent non-fatal warning.

verdict.reasoning: The 'Unknown' default is real code (portfolio_manager.py:272, :319, :360-362 all confirmed), but the claimed defect — enrichment failure hard-freezing the book at 2 positions and suppressing diversification (S2) — does not occur. Four reasons: (1) _fetch_ticker_meta is internally fail-soft — its BQ leg (paper_trading.py:1141-1142) and every yfinance worker (:1163-1167) are individually try/excepted with WARN+continue, so it returns a PARTIAL dict rather than raising; the outer except at autonomous_loop.py:760 that the finding hangs its scenario on rarely fires. (2) A candidate has THREE sector fallbacks (screener -> full_report.market_data.sector -> analysis.sector, portfolio_manager.py:221-227), so it only becomes 'Unknown' if all three are empty — a far broader outage than 'ticker_meta degraded.' (3) Existing positions read their sector as a PERSISTED column from paper_positions (not re-fetched each cycle), and empty-sector rows are re-enriched every cycle (autonomous_loop.py:1198-1226), so held positions with real sectors (finding admits AMD='Technology') never collapse into 'Unknown' on a transient outage. (4) Decisive: the direction of effect is backwards.
