With held positions in their real buckets and new candidates lumped into 'Unknown', the 'Unknown' bucket starts at 0 and the cap PERMITS up to max_per_sector=2 additional 'Unknown' BUYs on top of the real-sector positions — the book GROWS (2 -> ~4), it does not freeze. The 'freeze at 2 across all true sectors' outcome requires all held positions to also be 'Unknown', which contradicts the finding's own observation and is reachable only via a sustained total BQ+yfinance outage that would also starve the screen/analyze pipeline and produce 0 candidates (no buys regardless of the cap). The residual (excluding unclassified names from the count cap) is at most a minor robustness nicety, not a bug that explains the operator's stuck-at-2 symptom — hence P3, real=false.

refuted.title: DOMINANT throughput killer: analysis stage emits HOLD/score-0 for ~98% of candidates, so the BUY-only gate drops nearly everything
refuted.summary: decide_trades only builds a buy candidate when rec is BUY/STRONG_BUY (`if rec not in _BUY_RECS: continue`), but the upstream analysis stage returns HOLD/final_score=0 for almost every ticker, so the buy funnel is near-empty every cycle. This is the proximate cause of 'Executing 0 trades'.
refuted.failure_scenario: BQ analysis_results 2026-07-06..07-13: 30+ HOLD rows with final_score=0.0 vs only 2 BUY (AMD score6, MU score8). paper_portfolio_snapshots: trades_today=0 on nearly every day since 2026-06-11 (only 2026-07-09 had 2). The 5-7 tickers analyzed each cycle (MU/AMD/DDOG/SNDK/DELL/INTC/000660.KS/009150.KS) all return HOLD -> zero buy candidates enter the loop -> 0 trades. The gate at :183 is correct; the defect is the all-HOLD/score-0 analysis output it faithfully forwards (which should be tripping the phase-56.2 degraded-scoring guard every cycle).
refuted.fix_sketch: Instrument decide_trades to log the candidate funnel counts (analyzed -> BUY_recs -> sector-blocked -> executed) per cycle. Then attack the root: the analysis stage is producing HOLD/score-0 en masse (see rail/parse-fail finding). Confirm the degraded-scoring P1 alarm is actually reaching the operator (webhook paging is dead per memory) so all-HOLD cycles are visible instead of silent.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding is filed as a code defect at backend/services/portfolio_manager.py:183 but explicitly concedes that line is correct ("The gate at :183 is correct"). Verification confirms this: line 183 (`if rec not in _BUY_RECS: continue`, with _BUY_RECS={"BUY","STRONG_BUY"} at :58 and case-normalization at :177) is a correct BUY-only filter, and it provably does NOT drop genuine BUYs. BQ financial_reports.analysis_results (2026-07-01+) shows exactly 2 BUY rows (avg_score 7.0), and both (AMD/MU) executed as trades on 2026-07-09 per the finding's own evidence — every genuine BUY in the window passed the gate. Zero false negatives.

The finding identifies no defect at the cited location. Its fix_sketch is pure observability ("instrument decide_trades to log the funnel"), and it defers the actual root cause to a SEPARATE finding ("see rail/parse-fail finding"). A throughput finding whose cited line is admittedly correct and whose real cause is another finding is a mislabeled symptom description, not a code defect at portfolio_manager.py:183.

The upstream all-HOLD/score-0 phenomenon is real in BQ (46/52 HOLDs score-0) but (a) is the analysis stage's output, not a bug at :183; (b) is ambiguous between legitimate no-edge verdicts and degraded output — system memory documents ~66% cash as rational and alt-strategy all-neutral labels as a KNOWN characteristic, not a defect; (c) is already instrumented — the phase-56.2 degraded-scoring guard (_degraded_scoring_check at autonomous_loop.py:1959) fires a P1 alarm when >=3/all analyses score 0, the exact mechanism the finding says "should be tripping"; and (d) the synthetic-0.0/HOLD rail defect has a dedicated flag-gated fix (paper_synthesis_integrity_enabled, phase-61.2).

So the cited code is correct and empirically validated; the real concern is a distinct, already-tracked upstream issue with an existing detection guard. As filed against :183, the finding cannot be confirmed. corrected_severity=P3 because the only residual actionable content is a nice-to-have funnel-logging enhancement (already partly covered by the position-cap log at :346, the trade-decisions log at :493, and the degraded-scoring guard), not a bug fix.

refuted.title: Successful BUYs deploy only ~3% of NAV: lite RiskJudge caps position_pct at 1-10 and defaults/reduces to ~3%, leaving the book ~97% cash
refuted.summary: The lite RiskJudge prompt asks for recommended_position_pct on a 1-10 scale (:1777) and _LITE_RISK_DEFAULT is 3.0 (:1799). decide_trades sizes target_amount = nav*(position_pct/100) (portfolio_manager.py:380), so a typical APPROVE_REDUCED verdict buys ~3% of NAV and the hard ceiling is 10%. Even when trades fire, capital deployment is tiny -> low throughput of capital and poor diversification.
refuted.failure_scenario: BQ: the only two recent buys (AMD, MU on 2026-07-09) both carry risk_judge_decision=APPROVE_REDUCED; AMD cost_basis $719.93 and MU $839.92 on a ~$24,000 NAV = ~3.0-3.5% each. paper_portfolio_snapshots show positions_value ~$600-1,500 while cash sits at ~$23,000 (2.5-6% deployed). The 1-10 cap means max per position is ~10% of NAV, structurally preventing the book from being invested. Also `float(risk_dict.get('recommended_position_pct') or DEFAULT)` at :2329-2332 turns a genuine 0.0 into 3.0 (falsy-zero), and portfolio_manager :379 `position_pct or 10.0` similarly bumps 0 to 10% (both raise size, not suppress, but confirm the sizing path is fragile).
refuted.fix_sketch: Raise the RiskJudge sizing scale (e.g., allow up to 20-25% for high-conviction APPROVE_FULL) or add a separate conviction-driven base size, so BUY/STRONG_BUY with high confidence deploy meaningful capital. Track deployed-capital % as a first-class throughput metric.

verdict.reasoning: The mechanical citations are accurate (lite prompt 1-10 at autonomous_loop.py:1777; _LITE_RISK_DEFAULT=3.0 at :1799; target_amount=nav*pct/100 at portfolio_manager.py:380; falsy-zero `or DEFAULT` at :2329-2332 and `position_pct or 10.0` at :379), and ~3% deployment per BUY does occur. But it is NOT a defect: (1) The 2-10% scale is the intended conservative risk-sizing ladder, defined explicitly in the FULL-path skill backend/agents/skills/risk_judge.md:43 (APPROVE_FULL=5-10%, APPROVE_REDUCED=2-5%) and risk_debate.py:298 default 3. Since context says lite mode is OFF, the FULL orchestrator path runs, so the cited lite constants aren't even the operative path — and the full path shares the same by-design small sizing. (2) There is no hard 10% code ceiling; decide_trades (portfolio_manager.py:377-381) applies no numeric clamp — the ceiling is a soft prompt instruction, so 'hard ceiling is 10%' is wrong. (3) Per-position sizing is not the operative cause of the ~97% cash symptom: the buy loop (portfolio_manager.py:339-451) iterates over ALL buy_candidates deploying nav*pct/100 each until available_cash/max_positions(20) exhausts, so with 20 candidates it would deploy ~100%. The book stays ~97% cash because only ~2 tickers clear the upstream gates (few candidates) — a candidate-throughput issue, not sizing. Even maxing per-position sizing at 10%, 2 positions = ~20% deployed / ~80% cash, so the proposed fix would not resolve the symptom. (4) Position SIZE != trade COUNT, so it doesn't explain S3 (number of trades). (5) The falsy-zero sub-claims RAISE sizing (0->3%, 0->10%), the opposite of suppression, which the finding itself concedes. The North-Star memory also records ~66% cash as historically rational. Net: real code behavior, but intended conservative sizing that misattributes the low-deployment/low-throughput symptoms; at most a P3 tuning observation, not a P1 defect.

refuted.title: Trading-settings sector/position caps are silently overridden by the risk_overrides shadow surface
refuted.summary: The live decide_trades loop reads paper_max_per_sector and paper_max_positions via risk_overrides.get_effective(...), which returns any active in-memory override in preference to the settings value the Trading-settings UI writes to .env — so a saved UI change can have zero effect on trading with no user feedback.
refuted.failure_scenario: An operator (or an away-ops / Slack automation / the /api/paper-trading/risk-limits SET_RISK_LIMIT route at paper_trading.py:602) sets an override, e.g. paper_max_per_sector=2. It persists to handoff/risk_overrides_audit.jsonl and the process-global _overrides dict (risk_overrides.py:152). Later the operator opens Paper Trading > Trading settings, changes 'Max positions per sector' to 4, clicks Save. settings_api.py:431-447 writes PAPER_MAX_PER_SECTOR=4 to .env, clears the get_settings cache, and the PUT returns FullSettings showing 4, so the UI reports success and displays 4. But portfolio_manager.py:304 calls risk_overrides.get_effective('paper_max_per_sector', 4) which returns the override 2 (get_effective returns the stored override whenever the key is present, risk_overrides.py:139-141), so the live loop still caps sectors at 2. Identical clobber for 'Max simultaneous positions' at portfolio_manager.py:337. The two surfaces are never reconciled: the settings PUT does not clear the matching override, and the risk-limits surface is not surfaced in the settings UI. Anchor confirms the override audit file is currently empty, so this is latent today — but whenever an override for one of these keys exists it is effectively P0 for that key (the UI setting becomes a no-op). This is a concrete, code-cited instance of the 'two settings surfaces diverge/clobber' failure and a strong candidate root cause for S1.
refuted.fix_sketch: Reconcile the two surfaces: on PUT /api/settings/ for a key that is also a risk_overrides ALLOWED_KEY (paper_max_per_sector, paper_max_per_sector_nav_pct, paper_max_positions), clear any active override for that key (risk_overrides.clear_override) so the UI write wins, OR surface the effective (override-adjusted) value in FullSettings and warn in the Trading-settings UI when an override is shadowing a field. Add a test asserting a settings PUT changes get_effective() for these keys.

verdict.reasoning:
The finding describes a real code mechanism in the abstract but the defect cannot be occurring in the running system and does not explain symptom S1.

VERIFIED CODE FACTS:
1. Override store is empty. handoff/risk_overrides_audit.jsonl does NOT exist (ls -> "No such file or directory"). RiskOverrideState._load_from_audit returns immediately when the file is absent (risk_overrides.py:95-96), so _overrides = {}. get_effective(key, default) therefore hits line 141 (return default) for every key; the override-preference branch (line 140, return self._overrides[key]) is dead in the current process. The live decide_trades loop at portfolio_manager.py:304 and :337 receives exactly settings.paper_max_per_sector / settings.paper_max_positions, UNSHADOWED. So a saved UI setting is NOT clobbered today.
2. No UI can set an override. grep of frontend/src for risk-limits / SET_RISK_LIMIT / set_override / override_value returns zero hits. The only writer is PUT /api/paper-trading/risk-limits (paper_trading.py:595-612), confirmation-gated on the literal string SET_RISK_LIMIT and not wired to any frontend component. The operator's Trading-settings/manage page (manage/page.tsx:238) edits paper_max_per_sector through the GLOBAL settings surface, never the override surface. An override could only appear via a hand-crafted curl or an away-ops/Slack automation, and the absent audit file proves none has run.
3. Override-wins-over-settings is documented, intended design, not a bug. risk_overrides.py:1-26 states the knobs are read at-decide-time and picked up by the next cycle without restart, deliberately modeled on the kill-switch break-glass pattern, with "cleared means revert to the settings default." Precedence over the settings default is the module's explicit contract, analogous to a kill switch outranking normal config; an operator setting an override is deliberately choosing the higher-priority value.

CONCLUSION: The finding's own text concedes it is "latent today." Because (a) the precondition (an active override) does not hold, (b) the path is unreachable from the UI the operator uses, and (c) the precedence is intended, audited break-glass behavior, this is NOT a strong candidate root cause for S1. With no override set (the actual state), a UI change to Max Positions Per Sector flows straight through to the live loop and is not shadowed by this path at all. At most it is a P3 UX-reconciliation nicety (two operator surfaces could theoretically diverge only after a deliberate, audited, non-UI override), not the claimed P2 correctness defect, and it does not explain the reported live symptom. Hence real=false, verdict REFUTED.

refuted.title: risk_overrides.get_effective shadows the Settings-page value for paper_max_per_sector / paper_max_positions — a stale override makes the setting a silent no-op
refuted.summary: decide_trades reads the sector cap and position cap via risk_overrides.get_effective("paper_max_per_sector", settings.paper_max_per_sector) (line 303-305) and get_effective("paper_max_positions", ...) (line 336-338). If an override was ever set through POST /api/paper-trading/risk-overrides (risk_overrides.py:148, persisted to handoff/risk_overrides_audit.jsonl and replayed on restart), it PERMANENTLY wins over any edit made on the global Settings page — the .env/get_settings value is ignored until the override is explicitly cleared.
refuted.failure_scenario: Operator (or a prior session/UI) sets a paper_max_per_sector or paper_max_positions override. Later the operator changes MAX POSITIONS PER SECTOR / MAX SIMULTANEOUS POSITIONS on the Settings (or Paper Trading) page; settings_api PUT correctly writes .env and clears the lru_cache, and the scheduler DOES re-read fresh settings each cycle (paper_trading.py:1327 + autonomous_loop:181) — but decide_trades still returns the override value, so the change appears to have 'no effect'. This is exactly the S1 symptom shape. The override audit file is reportedly empty right now (so likely not the current live cause), but it is a live footgun: there is no UI surfacing of an active override next to the setting, and the enrichment gate at autonomous_loop:1197 reads settings directly while the cap reads the override, so the two can silently disagree.
refuted.fix_sketch: Surface any active risk-override prominently in the Settings/Paper-Trading UI and warn (or auto-clear) when the operator edits the same key on the settings page; or make the Settings-page write also clear the corresponding override. At minimum, log at decide-time when an override is shadowing a just-changed setting.

verdict.reasoning: The get_effective shadow MECHANISM is code-real (risk_overrides.py:134-141; portfolio_manager.py:303-305, 336-338 read both caps through it), but the reported P2 defect does not manifest. (1) No override exists: handoff/risk_overrides_audit.jsonl is absent, so _load_from_audit returns immediately (risk_overrides.py:95-96), _overrides stays empty, and get_effective returns the settings default for both keys — zero shadowing today. The finding concedes this, so it does not explain S1. (2) No path to silently create one: grep of frontend/src shows zero risk-overrides/risk-limits references (no UI to set one, contradicting the finding's "prior session/UI set it"); backend-wide, set_override is called only from the confirmation-gated PUT /api/paper-trading/risk-limits route (paper_trading.py:599-602, requires confirmation=="SET_RISK_LIMIT") and the module wrapper — nothing in the loop, startup, or migrations auto-sets it. (3) The override-wins-over-settings behavior is the explicit, documented phase-49.1 feature (module docstring: read at-decide-time, no restart; "'cleared' means revert to the settings default"), i.e. intended, bounded, audited, clearable design — not a defect. (4) Not silent: GET /api/paper-trading/risk-limits returns settings_default, effective_value, and overridden per key (paper_trading.py:578-592), so an active override is fully queryable via API. Finding also misidentifies the route (claims POST /risk-overrides at risk_overrides.py:148; that line is the set_override method def, and the real route is PUT /risk-limits). The only residual is a discoverability nit — settings_api PUT does not clear a manually-set API override and there is no frontend surfacing — but triggering it requires a deliberate, confirmation-gated, UI-less API call that has never occurred and is visible via the GET endpoint. That is P3 polish, not P2 money-path correctness, and it explains none of S1/S2/S3.

refuted.title: Trading settings are discoverable only via a small unlabeled-context gear, gated behind isInitialized
refuted.summary: Per phase-44.2 the Manage/Trading-settings tab was removed from the paper-trading tablist; the only entry point is a small 'Settings' gear Link that renders solely when isInitialized is true, so the sector-cap and max-positions controls are buried and conditionally hidden.
refuted.failure_scenario: Before paper trading is initialized (or if the isInitialized status read is stale/false), the gear Link at layout.tsx:391-400 is not rendered and the settings tab is absent from TABS, leaving no visible path to change Max positions per sector / Max simultaneous positions. Even when shown, the control is a single 16px gear among the header actions rather than a tab, which contributes to the operator perceiving the setting as unchangeable/hard to reach.
refuted.fix_sketch: Restore a labeled 'Settings' entry in the tablist (or a persistent, clearly labeled button that is not gated on isInitialized), so the trading-settings surface is always reachable.

verdict.reasoning: Refuted against the code in frontend/src/app/paper-trading/layout.tsx:391-400. (1) The finding's core symptom "small unlabeled-context gear" is false: the entry point is a full padded button (px-3 py-2 text-sm font-medium) containing BOTH a visible Settings text label AND aria-label="Paper trading settings + deposit". The "16px" is only the Gear glyph inside a labeled button, not the clickable target. (2) The failure scenario is inverted. isInitialized = status?.status !== "not_initialized" (line 215); when status is null/stale, status?.status is undefined and undefined !== "not_initialized" is TRUE, so the Settings button renders optimistically during loading/stale reads. It is hidden ONLY when the backend explicitly reports status === "not_initialized" (genuinely no fund) — a state that also shows an Initialize-Fund CTA and an explanatory empty state, and in which there are no positions/NAV to configure. So a stale/false read makes the path MORE visible, not hidden — the opposite of the claimed defect. (3) It doesn't explain the operator's symptoms. The live fund IS initialized (2 positions, 60 trades), so isInitialized is definitively true and the Settings button renders; the /paper-trading/manage route exists (page.tsx present) and hosts editable PaperSettingNum controls for paper_max_positions ("Max simultaneous positions", line 237) and paper_max_per_sector ("Max positions per sector", line 238). The settings surface is reachable and labeled, so this cannot be the cause of S1 ("can't change max positions per sector"). Gating a fund-configuration surface behind an initialized fund is defensible design, not a bug. Finding's own confidence is low; kept at P3 floor since even as a polish observation the characterization is materially inaccurate.
